topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with judgment as I stared at the roster posted on the corkboard. My name wasn't there. A cold, tight knot began to form just beneath my sternum, squeezing the air from my lungs with every shallow breath. I replayed the tryouts in my mind—the perfect passes, the explosive sprints—and suddenly, they felt hollow, like echoes in an empty hall. When Coach Miller finally called the names, the sound of my own voice was barely a whisper, lost in the vast space of disappointment settling over me like a heavy, damp blanket. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, and walked out, the noise of the other players’ excited chatter sounding impossibly loud and distant.

[story 2]

"It's fine," I managed to say, forcing a lightness into my tone that felt brittle and fake. My hands were clammy, gripping the strap of my duffel bag so tightly my knuckles were white. Every glance from the team captain felt like a physical prod, sharp and unwelcome. I tried to focus on the worn leather of my cleats, tracing the stitching with my thumb, anything to avoid meeting anyone's eyes. Inside, a churning wave of unfairness washed over me, making my stomach clench with a dull, persistent ache. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of shame that burned hotter than any physical exertion. I just wanted to disappear into the cool, sterile hallway.

[story 3]

The silence in the waiting room was deafening. I kept running the scenario over and over: the perfect game-winning shot, the unanimous selection. But the reality was a stark, unwelcome contrast. When the coach finally announced the final lineup, the words seemed to swim before my eyes. I felt a strange, heavy pressure building behind my eyes, a sensation that made it hard to focus on anything tangible. I stood there, my posture collapsing inward, shoulders hunched as if trying to shield myself from an invisible blow. A tremor ran through my legs, and I quickly moved toward the exit, needing the open air to swallow the weight of that sudden, crushing letdown.

A cold, tight knot began to form just beneath my sternum, squeezing the air from my lungs with every shallow breath. I replayed the tryouts in my mind—the perfect passes, the explosive sprints—and suddenly, they felt hollow, like echoes in an empty hall. When Coach Miller finally called the names, the sound of my own voice was barely a whisper, lost in the vast space of disappointment settling over me like a heavy, damp blanket. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, and walked out, the noise of the other players’ excited chatter sounding impossibly loud and distant.

"It's fine," I managed to say, forcing a lightness into my tone that felt brittle and fake. My hands were clammy, gripping the strap of my duffel bag so tightly my knuckles were white. Every glance from the team captain felt like a physical prod, sharp and unwelcome. I tried to focus on the worn leather of my cleats, tracing the stitching with my thumb, anything to avoid meeting anyone's eyes. Inside, a churning wave of unfairness washed over me, making my stomach clench with a dull, persistent ache. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of shame that burned hotter than any physical exertion. I just wanted to disappear into the cool, sterile hallway.

The silence in the waiting room was deafening. I kept running the scenario over and over: the perfect game-winning shot, the unanimous selection. But the reality was a stark, unwelcome contrast. When the coach finally announced the final lineup, the words seemed to swim before my eyes. I felt a strange, heavy pressure building behind my eyes, a sensation that made it hard to focus on anything tangible. I stood there, my posture collapsing inward, shoulders hunched as if trying to shield myself from an invisible blow. A tremor ran through my legs, and I quickly moved toward the exit, needing the open air to swallow the weight of that sudden, crushing letdown.
